1: Eliphaz’s third speech is the most directly accusatory in the book. As the theological accuser, Eliphaz occupies the structural role of the adversary in the divine council (Job 1:6–12): both challenge the reality of Job’s righteousness. In the NT, the adversary’s accusation finds its ultimate answer in Christ’s atoning work: Revelation 12:10 (“the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down, who accuses them before our God day and night”) names the overthrown adversary, and v11 (“they have conquered him by the blood of the Lamb”) names the mechanism of victory. Eliphaz’s accusations against Job are structurally the false accusations that Christ’s blood permanently defeats.
2: The question “can a man be profitable to God?” probes the transactional foundation of religion. The NT answer is not that humans are profitable to God, but that God in Christ makes humans “acceptable” to himself: Ephesians 1:6 (“to the praise of his glorious grace, with which he has blessed us in the Beloved”). The value humans have before God is not intrinsic (Eliphaz is right about this) but derivative — the value Christ imparts through union. In Christ, the unprofitable servant becomes the adopted son.
3: “Is it any pleasure to the Almighty if you are in the right?” — the question of divine pleasure in human righteousness is answered definitively in Christ: Matthew 3:17 (“this is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased”); Job 17:5 (the same at the Transfiguration). The Father’s pleasure in the Son is the prototype and ground of his pleasure in those united to the Son. Eliphaz is asking the right question without access to the christological answer: the Almighty’s pleasure in human righteousness is mediated through his pleasure in Christ.
4: The ironic question — is it because of your piety that God reproves you? — creates the exact structure of the prologue: God’s acknowledgment of Job’s piety (Job 1:8) leading to his permission of the test. Eliphaz uses the question satirically; the prologue uses it seriously. In the NT, Jesus is similarly tested: Luke 4:1–13 (the temptation immediately follows the divine declaration of pleasure in Job 3:22). The beloved Son in whom the Father is well pleased is also the one immediately tested in the wilderness.
5: “Is not your wickedness great? There is no end to your iniquities” — Eliphaz applies to Job the charge that belongs to sinners generally. Isaiah 53:12: Christ “was numbered with the transgressors” — the innocent one bearing the transgressors’ accusation. Eliphaz voices the false accusation that the cross takes seriously at its deepest level: the sin was not Job’s, but Christ genuinely bore what was not his (2 Corinthians 5:21: “he made him to be sin who knew no sin”). The accusation that misidentifies the guilty party is the type of the accusation that was correctly applied to Christ as the sin-bearer.
6: The specific false accusations — taking pledges from brothers for nothing, stripping naked the weary — contrast with Christ’s actual works: he gave rather than took; he clothed the naked (the woman with the issue of blood found wholeness rather than shame). Luke 4:18 (“he has sent me to proclaim release to the captives”) is the programmatic reversal of Eliphaz’s accusation against Job: where Eliphaz claims Job took from the poor, Christ’s mission is to give to the poor.
7: Eliphaz’s accusation that Job withheld water from the weary and bread from the hungry inverts Christ’s actual ministry: John 4:10 (Jesus offers living water); John 6:35 (“I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst”). The false accusation against Job that he denied water and bread is answered by Christ who gives the water and bread that fully satisfy.
8: The contrast between the powerful man who occupies the land and the widow/orphan who is pushed aside is reversed in Christ’s kingdom: Matthew 5:3 (“blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven”); Luke 1:52–53 (“he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty”). The social structure that Eliphaz accuses Job of exploiting is precisely the structure Christ’s kingdom overturns.
9: The widows sent away empty and orphans crushed — the paradigmatic crimes against the socially vulnerable — are the crimes Christ’s entire ministry implicitly reverses. Matthew 15:21–28 (the Syrophoenician widow’s daughter healed); Mark 12:42–43 (the widow’s offering honored above all). The widow who is sent away empty is, in Christ’s ministry, the widow who is received and whose offering is celebrated. The orphan is the one to whom Christ says “I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you” (John 14:18).
10: Snares surrounding Job because of his supposed wickedness — the retributive reading of suffering that the passion narrative definitively overturns. The snares and terrors at Gethsemane and Golgotha surrounded the only truly innocent person in history; the retributive reading would require that Christ was uniquely wicked. The absurdity of that conclusion reveals the absurdity of applying the snares-equals-wickedness formula to Job.
11: Darkness and floods overwhelming the wicked — the judgment categories that Eliphaz applies to Job. The darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45: three hours of darkness) is the sign that judgment has indeed fallen — but on the innocent one who bore the guilty’s penalty. The darkness that should not be Job’s fell on Christ; Christ’s darkness-free resurrection light is what Job will ultimately receive (the divine speeches and epilogue).
12: God high in the heavens, the stars at their height — Eliphaz describes divine transcendence to establish human smallness. Philippians 2:6–7 reverses this vertically: the one who existed in the form of God (“the heights of heaven”) “emptied himself, taking the form of a servant.” The divine transcendence that Eliphaz uses to crush Job’s legal pretensions is the height from which Christ voluntarily descended to be present in human suffering.
13: The charge that Job thinks God cannot see through the thick clouds — a theology of divine distance — is answered by the Incarnation: John 1:14 (“the Word became flesh and dwelt among us”). The God who Eliphaz describes as distant behind clouds is the God who in Christ came as close as possible, dwelling in the same flesh that Job inhabits. The divine hiddenness that Eliphaz uses as an argument becomes the divine nearness of the Incarnation.
14: “Thick clouds veil him, so that he does not see, and he walks on the vault of heaven.” John 1:14: “we have seen his glory.” The cloud-veiled God is the God who unveiled himself in Christ: Colossians 1:15 (“he is the image of the invisible God”); John 14:9 (“whoever has seen me has seen the Father”). The invisibility that Eliphaz uses to dismiss Job’s case is the very invisibility that the Incarnation permanently dissolves: God has become visible in the face of Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
15: The old path of the wicked who were swept away anticipates the NT teaching on the two ways: Matthew 7:13–14 (the broad road leading to destruction and the narrow road leading to life). Christ is the narrow road itself (John 14:6: “I am the way”); those who choose the old path of the wicked choose the path that was judged in the flood and will be judged again at the eschaton.
16: The wicked snatched away before their time, their foundation poured out as a stream — the sudden collapse of those who built on the wrong foundation. Matthew 7:24–27: the house on sand falls, the house on rock (Christ as the cornerstone, Ephesians 2:20) stands. The poured-out foundation of the wicked is the anti-type of Christ as the foundation that cannot be moved (1 Corinthians 3:11: “no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ”).
17: The wicked saying “depart from us” to God anticipates the eschatological judgment: Matthew 7:23 (“depart from me, I never knew you”). The direction of the “depart” is reversed: the wicked who said depart to God will hear “depart” from the Son of Man as judge. The demand to be left alone from God is granted eschatologically in a way that is the ultimate loss.
18: Yet God filled their houses with good things — the paradox of divine generosity toward those who reject him. Matthew 5:45 (“he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust”): the common grace of divine goodness is extended even to those who say “depart from us.” This common grace is ultimately aimed at repentance (Romans 2:4: “do you not know that God’s kindness is meant to lead you to repentance?”).
19: The righteous seeing and rejoicing at the wicked’s judgment — the eschatological vindication of the innocent. Revelation 19:1–3: the heavenly multitude cries “Hallelujah! Salvation and glory and power belong to our God, for his judgments are true and just.” The rejoicing of the righteous at divine judgment is not schadenfreude but the recognition that justice has finally been done — the vindication that Job has been longing for throughout the dialogue.
20: Their abundance destroyed and fire consuming their remnant — the complete judgment on those who rejected the divine offer. Hebrews 10:27: “a fearful expectation of judgment, and a fury of fire that will consume the adversaries.” The fire that consumes the remnant of the wicked is the same fire that purifies and refines the righteous (1 Corinthians 3:13–15: the fire that tests each one’s work). Christ bore the consuming fire of divine judgment so that those who trust him are refined but not consumed.
21: “Agree with God and be at peace; thereby good will come to you” — Eliphaz unknowingly describes the gospel: Romans 5:1 (“since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ”). The peace that Eliphaz offers through moral reform and submission, the NT offers through Christ’s atoning work. The structure is the same (agree with God → peace); the mechanism is entirely different: not human penitential effort but Christ’s righteousness credited to the believer.
22: Receiving instruction from God’s mouth and placing his words in the heart — the Torah-internalization that Eliphaz recommends is the structure of the new covenant: Jeremiah 31:33 (“I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts”); Hebrews 8:10 (the new covenant promise fulfilled in Christ). The external law becomes internal by the Spirit; what Eliphaz prescribes as a human moral achievement, the new covenant delivers as divine gift through Christ.
23: “If you return to the Almighty you will be built up” — Eliphaz’s restoration formula. 1 Peter 2:5: “you yourselves like living stones are being built up as a spiritual house.” The building-up (tibaneh) that Eliphaz offers through repentance, the NT delivers through union with Christ as the cornerstone. The removal of iniquity from the tent is completed in Christ through forgiveness and the Spirit’s sanctifying work.
24: Laying gold in the dust, treating Ophir gold as river-gravel — the detachment from wealth as the condition of genuine spiritual treasure. Matthew 6:19–21 (“do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth…but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven”); Matthew 13:44–46 (the man who sells all he has for the pearl of great value). Eliphaz calls for material detachment so that God becomes the treasure; Christ is the treasure for whom all earthly gold is gladly counted as refuse (Philippians 3:8: “I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord”).
25: “Let the Almighty be your gold and your precious silver” — God as the supreme treasure is the wisdom tradition’s highest aspiration (Psalms 73:25: “there is nothing on earth that I desire besides you”). In the NT, this supreme treasure is located specifically in Christ: Colossians 2:3 (“in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge”). The God-as-treasure that Eliphaz describes in abstract theological terms is identified concretely in Christ as the one in whom all divine treasure is found and accessible.
26: Delight in the Almighty and lifting the face to God — the restored intimacy of the forgiven. Hebrews 4:16: “let us then with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need.” The face lifted in confidence toward God is the access that Christ’s high-priestly work makes possible; the delight that Eliphaz prescribes as the goal of human penitential effort, Christ provides as the fruit of accomplished redemption.
27: Prayer to God with the promise of being heard — Eliphaz’s restoration promise. John 15:7: “if you abide in me, and my words abide in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you.” The conditions of heard prayer that Eliphaz describes (restored relationship, cleared conscience) are fulfilled in Christ’s work: 1 John 5:14 (“this is the confidence that we have toward him, that if we ask anything according to his will he hears us”). The hearing prayer that Eliphaz offers through moral performance, Christ delivers through union.
28: “You will decide on a matter and it will be established for you, and light will shine on your ways” — the extraordinary promise of effective prayer and divine guidance. John 15:16: “you did not choose me, but I chose you and appointed you that you should go and bear fruit, and so that whatever you ask the Father in my name, he may give it to you.” The decree that stands is the prayer offered in Christ’s name; the light on the ways is Christ himself who is the light (John 8:12: “I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness”).
29: When others are humbled you will say “there is lifting up,” for God saves the humble — the reversal of the proud. Luke 14:11: “everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.” James 4:6 (“God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble,” quoting Proverbs 3:34). This principle, which Eliphaz uses as the moral framework for Job’s restoration, is fulfilled in Christ’s humiliation and exaltation (Philippians 2:8–9: “he humbled himself…therefore God has highly exalted him”).
30: The innocent man delivered through the cleanness of Job’s hands — the intercessory function of the righteous. The ironically profound concluding verse of Eliphaz’s speech prefigures precisely what happens in the epilogue (Job 42:7–8): God commands the friends to go to Job, and Job will pray for them. Christ’s clean hands — his perfect righteousness — are the ground of intercession for the guilty (Hebrews 7:25: “he always lives to make intercession for them”). The innocent man whose clean hands deliver the guilty is the pattern of Christ’s substitutionary intercession.
